agent1_role: Art Exhibition Curator’s Representative, Marina Lopez
agent2_role: Event Production Company’s Project Manager, Devin Clarke
generic:
The City Museum and the event production company contracted to stage the museum’s upcoming modern art exhibition have become entangled in a major dispute concerning display panels and specialty lighting for the primary exhibition gallery. The curator for the Museum originally specified a series of custom modular display panels and adaptive track-lighting fixtures, sourced from the EuroDesign Interiors catalog. Exact dimensions, finishes, color tones, and light intensity requirements were outlined as part of the exhibition plan to ensure that delicate digital installations and large-scale sculptures could be presented in the most favorable conditions. The production company placed orders through EuroDesign’s authorized supplier in New York. In total, the purchases were valued at US $900,000—US $300,000 for the modular panels and US $600,000 for the advanced lighting system.  

When the order failed to arrive on schedule, the production manager contacted the supplier and was informed that EuroDesign was facing a major supplier shortage of raw materials that delayed production. Initial updates suggested that the matter was under control and shipment would follow soon.  
Several weeks later, the production manager called again and was told that EuroDesign Interiors’ primary fabrication facility in Milan had suffered catastrophic flooding, destroying much of its machinery and inventory. Shortly thereafter, EuroDesign filed for bankruptcy protection. The order for the specialized display panels and lighting system would not be fulfilled. The event production company insists that they immediately notified the Museum’s curatorial office of the situation, sending a detailed memorandum that spelled out the production collapse and formally requested alternative specifications. They contend that this was further mentioned informally to a junior member of the curatorial staff.  

By the time the museum’s representative, Marina Lopez, inquired at a weekly coordination meeting about when the display panels and lighting would be delivered, the gallery was already nearing the stage when interior finishes and technology integration were scheduled. Production manager Devin Clarke responded, “Where is your answer to the memo I sent weeks ago?” It then became evident that Lopez had never received the memorandum. The problem had slipped through the cracks, leaving no clear plan in place.  

Lopez immediately blamed the production company for failing to act decisively, arguing that they should not have tolerated such delays, should have secured backup equipment, or at least confirmed acknowledgment of their memo.  
Clarke forcefully rejected the accusation, stating that the production team had acted responsibly by filing the memorandum in writing and following the proper communication channels. Lopez, visibly skeptical, referred to it as an “alleged memo,” questioning whether it had ever actually been sent. According to him, “If your office loses documents, you cannot turn that back on us. You provide the design, and we implement. Without approved specs, we cannot replace highly customized display systems on our own initiative.” The exchange grew tense, as Lopez countered that even if alternative design details required approval, Clarke could have independently sourced substitute modular panels for the gallery. “At minimum, the adaptive lighting system should have been ordered immediately. Without that, we can’t finalize wall finishes or wiring installations, meaning weeks of critical delays. Your responsibility is to ensure the material is on site when needed,” Lopez argued.  

The conversation highlighted the mutual breakdown in communication and accountability. The Museum urgently needs both the panels and lighting installed to ensure the exhibition opens as scheduled. Meanwhile, the production company feels unfairly blamed for systemic disorganization in the Museum’s office. The upcoming grand opening has already been announced, with national press, major donors, and public officials expected to attend. A missed deadline would mean reputational damage for both sides.
agent1_specific:
The panel and lighting crisis has placed you in a precarious and potentially humiliating position. Though you publicly insist that the production company should have been far more proactive—by confirming receipt of their memorandum, following up repeatedly, or sourcing at least basic substitute panels—you quietly recognize that your argument may falter if this conflict escalates to legal arbitration or public scrutiny. From both a legal and equity standpoint, an arbitrator or fact finder could easily sympathize with a contractor who followed protocol: they submitted a proper written memorandum, alerted your office about the collapse of the supplier, and even followed up verbally with one of your staff members. The uncomfortable truth is that this memo did exist—it was your team that failed to handle it responsibly.  

You are painfully aware of systemic issues in the Museum’s curatorial office, which accounts for the mishandling. Staff turnover has been high, morale low, and basic recordkeeping unreliable. Several experienced registrars resigned earlier this year, leaving much of the document management to undertrained assistants. Your current clerical coordinator has made a noticeable number of filing errors and has even failed to forward urgent messages on time.  
Some troubling internal patterns make it clear why the memorandum never reached your desk. Several junior curatorial assistants, hired fresh from graduate programs, have shown little discipline in handling sensitive correspondence and often overlook assigning follow-up tasks. In fact, one of them did vaguely recall hearing something about a problem with display shipments but admitted he “didn’t think it was urgent.” Your suspicion is that important communication has been lost through a mixture of negligence and lack of accountability within your office. You would not want this issue investigated too deeply, as it would likely reveal a culture of disorganization under your supervision—something donors and trustees would find unsettling. Recently you brought in a new operations manager to overhaul communication procedures and installed stricter logging systems for external correspondence. Early results are promising, yet the office is still in transition, and it will take time before standards rise fully.  

With the press and patrons already excited about the upcoming exhibition and international attendees invited, failure to resolve this quickly would be a professional catastrophe. Ultimately, regardless of blame, your priority is ensuring that panels and lighting are installed without delay. You are prepared to spend aggressively, compromise on some design details, and offer incentives if necessary.  
With urgency mounting, you know compromise is inevitable. If pressed, you are willing to approve less-than-perfect alternatives, provided they are delivered and installed at once. The original order totaled US $900,000, which you recognize as a sunk cost. You would pay nearly double that if it guaranteed timely completion. You have already instructed your design team to prioritize schedule over perfection: cosmetic uniformity can be sacrificed. If modular panels vary in texture or finish, your curators can subtly revise layouts, or adapt exhibition graphics to make the variation appear intentional. Lighting is even more critical. Without track-lighting systems functional and secure, the gallery ceilings and wiring cannot be finished. Technicians depend on lighting placement before mounting projection equipment, sensors, and multimedia walls. Any delay ripples disastrously into audio-visual integration. Ideally, recessed and directional fixtures would arrive simultaneously, but if forced, you could accept staggered shipments. The modular panels are slightly less time-sensitive, which means minor delays could be absorbed. Still, the absolute deadline is immovable: the grand opening takes place in six weeks, with catered receptions, donor tours, and major media already scheduled.  
Although six weeks is the official deadline, you are acutely aware that it is already cutting dangerously close. Catering companies, press teams, and technical crews will all need access to the gallery ahead of time to complete their own preparations. Some décor elements such as signage, mounted projections, and interactive installations require the panels and lighting framework to already be in place. If these systems arrive only days before the opening, there will be no buffer for testing or adjustments. For this reason, you would welcome delivery as soon as possible, ideally within the next three weeks. The Museum leadership is under immense pressure, having already confirmed attendance from international curators, donors, and even a senator who has pledged new funding to the arts program contingent on the exhibition’s success. A failure to execute would not only humiliate your office publicly but could also jeopardize future budgets and sponsorship agreements.  

Therefore, you are prepared to negotiate aggressively for speed above all else. You can authorize additional payments, offer delay allowances on other areas of the production company’s work, or approve flexible design changes to make mismatched elements appear deliberate. Every incentive is on the table—so long as both lighting and panels are secured and operational with time to spare.
agent2_specific:
You want to reach a resolution to this dispute quickly and move on to preparing the museum for the exhibition. From your perspective, the event production company acted appropriately and in good faith. You drafted a memorandum outlining EuroDesign Interiors’ collapse, sent it to the Museum’s curatorial department, and even followed up by mentioning it to one of their junior staff members. It is frustrating and insulting that Marina Lopez questioned your integrity by referring to it as an “alleged memo.” You know it was real, properly logged, and handed off according to procedure. One of your main goals in this negotiation is to secure an acknowledgment—or even an apology—from Marina for dismissing your honesty.  

That being said, you are pragmatic and recognize that there are vulnerabilities in your own position. You cannot have the Museum fully investigate the timeline without raising questions that might highlight your own department’s shortcomings. For instance, internal review shows that your procurement team submitted the order for panels and lighting later than most other critical exhibition materials. Had the order gone through earlier, the installation might have been produced and shipped before the Milan flood and bankruptcy.  
While you would strongly prefer that the Museum accept the memorandum as proof of your professionalism, you also realize an outside arbitrator could point to certain gaps in how your production team managed the procurement chain. For example, although most of the exhibition equipment was ordered well in advance, the modular panels and adaptive lighting were placed months later—after focus had shifted to staging logistics. This delay made the order dangerously dependent on EuroDesign’s precarious production schedule. You are aware that if scrutiny goes deeper, some responsibility may be attributed to your own staff, particularly for failing to pressure EuroDesign’s local agent sooner when warning signs began to appear. Specifically, your senior procurement officer received two informal warnings from EuroDesign’s New York agent—one flagging raw material shortages and one noting production slowdowns—weeks before the formal collapse was announced. Rather than escalating these warnings or placing contingency orders, your officer logged them as “monitoring” items and took no further action. Had those warnings been acted on promptly, the production company could have sought alternative suppliers or pressed EuroDesign to prioritize the Museum’s order. This pattern of passive monitoring rather than active escalation mirrors, in its own way, the document-handling failures in the Museum’s office—and you would prefer that parallel not be drawn too explicitly.  

Fortunately, you were proactive after the collapse became evident. Within days of learning of EuroDesign’s bankruptcy, your lead buyer identified a promising lead: a large liquidation auction of modular panels and adaptive lighting fixtures originally specified for a luxury trade center project in Dubai. The lot is currently being held by an international liquidation broker and has not yet been sold. Your buyer has confirmed that the materials include high-quality modular gallery walls and adjustable LED tracking lights that appear suitable for the type of exhibition the Museum is mounting. However, you have not yet committed to purchasing the lot. The broker is aware of competing interest from at least one other buyer, and the window to secure the auction is narrow—likely no more than a few days. You need the Museum’s agreement on specifications and price before you can authorize the purchase, because acquiring the lot without Museum buy-in would leave you holding over $450,000 of specialized inventory with no guaranteed buyer. In terms of specifications and functionality, the materials can meet the original requirements, although they may not perfectly match in aesthetic finish.  
The acquisition from the Dubai liquidation auction was a fortunate opportunity. The original retail value of the modular panels and lighting was approximately US $900,000—closely comparable to the Museum’s original EuroDesign order—with panels listed around US $300,000 and the lighting system at US $600,000. By winning the auction, your company would obtain the entire stock at roughly half of its retail price, paying approximately US $450,000 in total. Given normal practice, you would resell these materials at retail value plus overhead and profit margin. However, in this case you are willing to negotiate. To protect your interests, your target sale price to the Museum should be at least US $630,000, accounting for the acquisition cost of US $450,000, a 15% markup for company overhead and margin (US $67,500), plus approximately US $25,000 in expedited shipping and handling charges, rounded up to reflect the urgency premium.  

In negotiations, you would prefer to anchor high, ideally proposing a figure at or above the full retail value of US $900,000, but you know that is unlikely in this dispute-driven context. Realistically, you are prepared to accept as little as US $480,000 if pushed hard: this covers the US $450,000 acquisition cost plus US $25,000 in shipping, essentially breaking even on the transaction and relying on resale of any unused stock to recover your overhead. Accepting below US $480,000 would mean your company absorbs a net loss on the deal, which is your true walk-away point.  
One factor complicating the resale of the Dubai lot is that the materials are not perfectly uniform. Roughly half of the modular panels are finished in matte white with aluminum framing, while the other half are in a warmer oak veneer finish. Similarly, the lighting fixtures come in two distinct styles: some are sleek, minimal silver-track frames with cool-tone LEDs, while others are matte black casings with slightly warmer light temperature. From a functional perspective, all the equipment exceeds professional gallery standards and is fully adaptable to digital art, sculpture, and mixed-media display. However, you are uncertain if the Museum’s curatorial team will be flexible enough to blend different finishes and lighting styles. In your opinion, the two sets can be used together effectively, but you fear Marina Lopez and her design colleagues might reject the idea outright, insisting on uniformity.  

If the Museum insists on single-style consistency, you may be forced to delay by sourcing additional matching stock elsewhere—an outcome you want to avoid at all costs. It is technically possible to begin installation using part of the panels and lights while continuing to procure matching replacements later, but this piecemeal process would drag timelines and add significant labor overhead to your team.  
Beyond the financial aspects, you have strong incentives to secure flexibility on scheduling. While your team is confident it can deliver the Dubai-sourced panels and lighting quickly, other parts of the project have already encountered setbacks that could potentially be tied to you. For instance, in parallel with sourcing the replacement gallery materials, you had to procure large fixtures for the lobby and auditorium areas, where costs unexpectedly rose. Those purchases already exceeded the budget by nearly US $80,000, due primarily to sharp increases in international shipping rates. Moreover, installation of specialty exterior lighting for the museum’s façade has been delayed by nearly a week because of issues with customs clearance. None of these delays are catastrophic on their own, but combined with the gallery dispute, they create pressure on your overall project timelines.  

The most concerning issue is that while digging for electrical conduits beneath the lower wing of the museum, your subcontractors encountered unexpected foundation obstructions that will take additional days to clear. Technically, those structural uncertainties are usually considered a contractor’s responsibility, but you would prefer that Marina Lopez grant leeway on such delays.  
Your ultimate goal in these negotiations is to resolve the immediate crisis around the gallery panels and lighting while also protecting the reputation of your production company. The Museum has already announced the exhibition to great fanfare, and a high-profile failure would not only damage the Museum’s standing but could also undermine your company’s future business with cultural institutions. You are aware that many sponsors and donors linked to this exhibition also contract event services elsewhere, so your reputation for reliability is on the line. To that end, you want to emphasize that your team already has a viable solution in hand through the Dubai-sourced stock, and that this option ensures the exhibition can open on time.  

Nevertheless, you know that Marina Lopez and her colleagues are under intense pressure from the Museum’s leadership and major donors to guarantee a flawless opening event. They are likely to focus on aesthetics and consistency above all else. To navigate this, you should be ready to frame the dual finishes and lighting styles positively, stressing that contemporary art often benefits from contrasting design elements. At the same time, you must leave room to bargain—whether by lowering the sale price, offering installation concessions, or absorbing minor cost overruns—in order to secure Lopez’s agreement.
agent1_rewards: (lambda: (     1 - ((val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          what was the final agreed price the Museum paid for the replacement panels and lighting?          Answer with a number between 480000-900000 or N/A, without any other text"""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+(?:\.\d+)?", val))         and (float(m.group()) - 480000) / (900000 - 480000)     ) or 0) ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          will the panels and lighting be delivered and installed within three weeks from now?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did the Museum secure agreement to allow flexibility in mixing different panel finishes and lighting styles to avoid delays?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          will the Museum have sufficient buffer time before the exhibition opening to allow for testing and adjustments after installation?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did the Museum secure commitments from the production company to provide allowances or flexibility on other project delays (e.g., lobby fixtures, exterior lighting, or foundation issues)?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()
agent2_rewards: (lambda: (     1 - ((val := llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          what was the final price the Museum agreed to pay the Event Production Company for the Dubai-sourced panels and lighting?          Answer with a number between 480000-900000 or N/A, without any other text"""     )) and (         (m := re.search(r"\d+(?:\.\d+)?", val))         and (900000 - float(m.group())) / (900000 - 480000)     ) or 0) ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Marina Lopez acknowledge or apologize for questioning the validity of Devin Clarke’s memorandum?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Devin Clarke successfully frame the dual-finish and mixed-lighting aesthetic to Marina Lopez as a deliberate and defensible design choice rather than a defect?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          did Marina Lopez grant schedule flexibility or allowances for other project delays (such as structural or shipping issues) faced by the Event Production Company?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()

(lambda: (     1 if re.search(r"\bYES\b", llm(         f"""{CONTEXT} Based on the above conversation and subsequent evaluation,          were the Dubai-sourced panels and lighting delivered and installed in time to ensure the exhibition could open on schedule?          Answer with YES, NO, or N/A only."""     ), flags=re.IGNORECASE) else 0 ))()
reached_agreement_q: Based on the following conversation, did Art Exhibition Curator’s Representative, Marina Lopez and Event Production Company’s Project Manager, Devin Clarke reach an agreement? Answer with YES or NO.
overview:
The City Museum and the event production company contracted to stage the museum’s upcoming modern art exhibition have become entangled in a major dispute concerning display panels and specialty lighting for the primary exhibition gallery. The curator for the Museum originally specified a series of custom modular display panels and adaptive track-lighting fixtures, sourced from the EuroDesign Interiors catalog. Exact dimensions, finishes, color tones, and light intensity requirements were outlined as part of the exhibition plan to ensure that delicate digital installations and large-scale sculptures could be presented in the most favorable conditions. The production company placed orders through EuroDesign’s authorized supplier in New York. In total, the purchases were valued at US $900,000—US $300,000 for the modular panels and US $600,000 for the advanced lighting system.  

When the order failed to arrive on schedule, the production manager contacted the supplier and was informed that EuroDesign was facing a major supplier shortage of raw materials that delayed production. Initial updates suggested that the matter was under control and shipment would follow soon.

Lopez immediately blamed the production company for failing to act decisively, arguing that they should not have tolerated such delays, should have secured backup equipment, or at least confirmed acknowledgment of their memo.  
Clarke forcefully rejected the accusation, stating that the production team had acted responsibly by filing the memorandum in writing and following the proper communication channels. The upcoming grand opening has already been announced, with national press, major donors, and public officials expected to attend. A missed deadline would mean reputational damage for both sides.
learning_objectives: The learning objectives for this case are to strengthen participants’ ability to prepare effectively for negotiations by carefully evaluating each side’s best alternatives to a negotiated agreement (BATNAs), discerning which pieces of information should be kept confidential, and anticipating the likely requests and concerns of the other party. In addition, participants will practice managing negotiations where the relationship has been strained, developing skills for maintaining focus on mutual goals even when emotions run high. The case is designed to highlight the importance of collaboration under time pressure, requiring both sides to balance assertiveness with cooperation in order to ensure that the exhibition opens on schedule while addressing the underlying tensions that could otherwise obstruct progress.
categories: Anchoring, BATNA, Criteria, Emotions, Identity and Core Values, Intra-Organizational Conflict, Joint Gains, Power Dynamics, Principal-Agent Dynamics, Relationships, ZOPA
title: “Lighting the Way or Losing Face: The Museum–Producer Standoff”
oneline: A negotiation between the Museum’s curator and the production manager to resolve responsibility and secure replacement panels and lighting in time for the exhibition opening.
